volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Epidicus (Section 1.1.90 - Section 2.1.175)

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: fídicinam emit , quam ipse amat , quam ábiens mandavit mihi .
sections.translation: a music-girl whom he was fond of , and whom on his departure he commissioned me about . He now , to please his fancy , has brought another one from the army . I ’ ve lost my hide , for when the old man finds out that he has been played tricks with , he ’ ll be flaying my back with twigs . But still , do you take all precautions .

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: át enim — bat enim , nihil est istuc . plane hoc corruptumst caput .
sections.translation: That ’ s of no use ! clearly this head of mine is addled ! You are a worthless fellow , Epidicus . ( In another tone .) What pleasure have you in being abusive ? Because you are forsaking yourself . What am I to do ? Do you ask me the question ? Why you yourself , in former days , were wont to lend advice to others .

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: aliquid aliqua reperiundumst . sed ego cesso ire obviam
sections.translation: Well , well ; something must be found out . But why delay to go meet the young man , that I may know how the matter stands ? And here he is himself . He is in a grave mood . He ’ s coming with Chaeribulus , his year ’ s-mate . I ’ ll step aside here , whence at my leisure I ’ ll follow their discourse .

sections.label: Section 1.2.104
sections.original: Rem tibi sum elocutus omnem , Chaeribule , atque admodum
sections.translation: I ’ ve told you all the matter , Chaeribulus , and

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: meorum maerorum atque amorum summam edictavi tibi .
sections.translation: I have fully disclosed to you the sum of my griefs and loves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.106
sections.original: Praeter aetatem et virtutem stultus es , Stratippocles .
sections.translation: You are foolish , Stratippocles , beyond your age and lineage . Does it shame you , because you have bought a captive girl , born of good family , from among the spoil ? Who will there be to impute it as a fault to you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.109
sections.original: Qui invident omnes inimicos mi illoc facto repperi ;
sections.translation: Through doing this , I ’ ve found that those who are envious are all enemies to me ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: at pudicitiae eius numquam nec vim nec vitium attuli .
sections.translation: but I ’ ve never offered [*] or criminal assault against her chastity .

sections.label: Section 1.2.111
sections.original: Iam istoc probior es meo quidem animo , cúm in amore temperes .
sections.translation: Then , so far , in my opinion at least , you are a still more deserving man , inasmuch as you are temperate in your passion .

sections.label: Section 1.2.112
sections.original: Nihil agit qui diffidentem verbis solatur suis ;
sections.translation: He effects nothing who consoles a desponding man with his words : he is a friend , who , in dubious circumstances , aids in deed when deeds are necessary .

sections.label: Section 1.2.114
sections.original: Quid tibi me vis facere ?
sections.translation: What do you wish me to do ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.114b
sections.original: Argenti dare quadraginta minas ,
sections.translation: To lend me forty minae of silver ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: quod danistae detur , unde ego illud sumpsi faenore .
sections.translation: to be paid to the Banker from whom I borrowed it on interest .

sections.label: Section 1.2.116
sections.original: Si hercle haberem ⋯
sections.translation: On my word , if I had it , I would not deny you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.116b
sections.original: Nam quid te igitur retulit
sections.translation: What then does it signify your being bounteous in talk , if all aid in the matter [*] is dead outright .

sections.label: Section 1.2.118
sections.original: Quin edepol egomet clamore differor , difflagitor .
sections.translation: Why faith , I myself am quite wearied and distracted with being dunned .

sections.label: Section 1.2.119
sections.original: Malim istius modi mihi amicos furno mersos quam foro .
sections.translation: I had rather my friends of that sort were thrust into a furnace than into litigation [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: sed operam Epidici nunc me emere pretio pretioso velim .
sections.translation: But now I could wish to buy me the assistance of Epidicus at a weighty price , a fellow whom I ’ ll hand over well-liquored with stripes to the baker [*] , unless he this day finds me forty minae before I ’ ve mentioned to him the last syllable of the sum .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124
sections.original: Salva res est : bene promittit , spero servabit fidem .
sections.translation: The matter ’ s all right ; he promises well ; he ’ ll keep faith , I trust .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: sine meo sumptu paratae iam sunt scapulis symbolae .
sections.translation: Without any expenditure of my own , an entertainment [*] is already provided for my shoulder-blades . I ’ ll accost the man . ( He goes up to STRATIPPOCLES .) The servant Epidicus wishes health to his master Stratippocles , on his arrival from abroad .

sections.label: Section 1.2.127b
sections.original: Vbi is est ?
sections.translation: Where is he ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.127c
sections.original: Adest .
sections.translation: Here he is ; I ’ m delighted that you have returned safe .

sections.label: Section 1.2.128b
sections.original: Tam tibi istuc credo quam mihi .
sections.translation: I believe you as much in that as I do myself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129
sections.original: Benene usque valuisti ?
sections.translation: Have you been well all along ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.129b
sections.original: A morbo valui , ab animo aeger fui .
sections.translation: I ’ ve been free from disease ; in mind I ’ ve been ailing .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: Quod ad me attinuit , ego curavi : quod mandavisti mihi
sections.translation: As regarded myself , I ’ ve taken care of what you entrusted to me ; it has been obtained ; the female captive has been bought , about which matter you were sending me letters so often .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132b
sections.original: Perdidisti omnem operam .
sections.translation: You ’ ve lost all your labour .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132c
sections.original: Nam qui perdidi ?
sections.translation: But why have I lost it ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.133
sections.original: Quia meo neque cara est cordi neque placet .
sections.translation: Because she is not dear to my heart , nor does she please me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133b
sections.original: Quid retulit
sections.translation: What means it , then , that you gave me such strict injunctions , and sent letters to me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: Illam amabam olím , nunc iam alia cura impendet pectori .
sections.translation: Formerly I did love her ; but now another passion influences my heart .

sections.label: Section 1.2.136
sections.original: Hercle miserum est ingratum esse hómini id quod facias bene .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , it is a shocking thing for that to be unpleasant for a man which you have managed well for him ; where I ’ ve done well , I ’ ve in reality done ill , since love has shifted sides .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138
sections.original: Desipiebam mentis , cum illa scripta mittebam tibi .
sections.translation: I wasn ’ t right in my mind when I sent those letters to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Men piacularem oportet fieri ob stultitiam tuam ,
sections.translation: Is it proper that I should be the atonement for your folly ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: ut meum tergum tuae stultitiae subdas succidaneum ?
sections.translation: so as for you to substitute my back as the scape-goat [*] for your folly ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.141
sections.original: Quid istic ? verba facimus . huic homini ópust quadraginta minis
sections.translation: Why are we making words about that ? This man ( pointing to himself ) has need of forty minae , ready money , and in all haste , for him to pay a Banker , and speedily too .

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: Dic modo unde auferre me vis . a quo trápezita peto ?
sections.translation: Only tell me from what quarter you wish me to get them . From what banker am I to seek them ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.144
sections.original: Vnde libet . nam ni ante solem occasum elo ⋯
sections.translation: From where you like . But if I don ’ t finger them before sunset ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: meam domum ne inbitas : tu te in pistrinum ⋯
sections.translation: don ’ t you enter my house ; off with yourself to the mill .

sections.label: Section 1.2.146
sections.original: Facile tu istuc sine periclo et cura , corde libero
sections.translation: You easily say that without risk and concern , and with a gay heart . I know our floggers ; I feel the pain when I ’ m beaten .

sections.label: Section 1.2.148
sections.original: Quid tu nunc ? patierin ut ego me interimam ?
sections.translation: How say you now ? Will you suffer me to destroy myself ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.148b
sections.original: Ne feceris .
sections.translation: Don ’ t do that . I ’ ll cope with this peril and bold attempt in preference .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: Nunc places , nunc ego te laudo .
sections.translation: Now you please me ; now do I commend you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150b
sections.original: Patiar ego istuc quod lubet .
sections.translation: I ’ ll submit to this in any way that ’ s pleasing to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.151
sections.original: Quid illa fiet fidicina igitur ?
sections.translation: What then is to be done about this music-girl ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.151b
sections.original: Aliqua res reperibitur ,
sections.translation: Some method shall be found out ; by some means I ’ ll disengage myself ; some way I shall get extricated .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152b
sections.original: Plenus consili es .
sections.translation: You are full of scheming ; I know you of old .

sections.label: Section 1.2.153b
sections.original: Est Euboicus miles locuples , multo auro potens ,
sections.translation: There is a rich Captain of Eubœa [*] , abounding in plenty of gold , who , when he knows that that one was bought for you , and that this other one has been brought here ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.156
sections.original: sed ubi illa est quam tu adduxisti tecum ?
sections.translation: will forthwith be entreating you , of his own accord , to transfer that other one to him . But where is she whom you have brought with you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.156b
sections.original: Iam faxo hic erit .
sections.translation: I shall have her here just now .

sections.label: Section 1.2.157
sections.original: Quid hic nunc agimus ?
sections.translation: What are we now doing here ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.157b
sections.original: Eamus intro huc ad te , ut hunc hodie diem
sections.translation: Let ’ s go in-doors here at your house , that , for the present , we may pass this day merrily .

sections.label: Section 1.2.158b
sections.original: Ite intro , ego de re argentaria
sections.translation: Go in-doors ; I ’ ll now call [*] a council in my heart to adopt measures about this money business ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.160
sections.original: quoi potissimum indicatur bellum unde argentum auferam .
sections.translation: against whom , in especial , war is to be declared , and out of whom I ’ m to get the money . Epidicus , consider what you are to do ; thus suddenly has this business been thrown upon you . But now you must not be slumbering , nor have you any leisure for delay . Now must you be daring ! ’ Tis my fixed determination to lay siege to the old man . I ’ ll go indoors ; I ’ ll tell the young man , my master ’ s son ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.165
sections.original: ne hinc foras ámbulet neve usquam óbviam veniat seni .—
sections.translation: not to walk abroad here , or come anywhere in the way of the old gentleman .

sections.label: Section 2.1.166
sections.original: Plérique homines , quós cum nil refért pudet ,
sections.translation: Mostly all men [*] are ashamed when they have no occasion to be ; when they ought to be ashamed , then does shame forsake them , when there ’ s a necessity for their being ashamed . That man , in fact , are you . What is there to be ashamed of

sections.label: Section 2.1.170
sections.original: génere natám bono paúperem domum
sections.translation: in your bringing home a wife , poor , but born of good family ? Especially her , whose daughter you say this girl is , who is at your house ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.173
sections.original: Révereor filium .
sections.translation: I have some regard for my son [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.1.173b
sections.original: At pól ego te crédidi
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , the wife whom you buried I thought you had felt some respect for ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.175
sections.original: cúius quotiéns sepulcrúm vides , sácruficas
sections.translation: whose tomb as oft as you see , you straightway sacrifice victims to Orcus ; and not without reason , in fact , since you ’ ve been allowed to get the better of her by surviving her .
